source: ESV Study Bible (Crossway) — Used under license
book_intro: The letter to the Hebrews was written to encourage Christians in a time of trial. It does so by focusing on the absolute supremacy and sufficiency of Jesus Christ. While God spoke in the past "many times and in many ways," he has now spoken to us "by his Son," Jesus Christ, who is the "exact imprint" of God's nature and who "upholds the universe by the word of his power" (1:1-3). Jesus accomplished complete salvation for all who trust in him (1:1-10:18). We dare not "neglect such a great salvation" (2:3; 5:12-6:20; 10:19-39). Rather, in our faith and in our everyday living, we should imitate the example of Christ and of those on the honor roll of faith (chs. 11-13). This letter, whose author is unknown, was probably written between a.d. 60 and 70.
chapter_intro: Chapter 7 is the theological center of Hebrews. The mysterious figure of Melchizedek, introduced in Gen 14 and recalled in Ps 110:4, becomes the basis for demonstrating that Jesus holds a priesthood categorically different from — and superior to — the Levitical order. Because Melchizedek appears in Genesis without genealogy, birth, or death, he serves as a type of the Son whose priesthood is 'by the power of an indestructible life.' The chapter argues that the very existence of a Melchizedekan priesthood implies the inadequacy of the Levitical system.

passages.title: The Greatness of Melchizedek
passages.text: Three details from Gen 14 establish Melchizedek's greatness: (1) Abraham, the ancestor of Levi, paid tithes to him; (2) Melchizedek blessed Abraham, and the lesser is always blessed by the greater; (3) unlike Levitical priests, Melchizedek has no recorded genealogy, birth, or death — making him a 'type' of the eternal Son.

passages.title: The Law Brought Nothing to Perfection
passages.text: The clinching argument: if perfection had been attainable through the Levitical priesthood, there would have been no need for God to promise a different kind of priest in Ps 110:4. The very existence of that prophecy is proof that the old system was preliminary. A change of priesthood entails a change of law — the whole Mosaic system is superseded, not merely supplemented.

passages.title: A Priest by Oath and Forever
passages.text: Levitical priests were appointed without an oath; Christ was appointed with the Father's sworn oath (Ps 110:4). Levitical priests were many and mortal; Christ is one and holds his priesthood permanently. Levitical priests offered sacrifice daily; Christ offered himself once for all. He is exactly the kind of high priest we need: holy, innocent, unstained, and now exalted above the heavens.

notes.text: king of righteousness … king of peace — The Hebrew meaning of Melchizedek's name and city (Salem = shalom) are taken as more than coincidence: they anticipate the Messianic King who brings both righteousness and peace.

without father or mother or genealogy — The argument is from the silence of the Genesis text, which the author takes as theologically significant: Melchizedek's unrecorded life typifies the eternal Son.

perfection — The Greek teleiōsis here means 'bringing to the goal,' accomplishing final atonement and access to God. The Levitical system could never do this permanently; it required endless repetition.
lives to make intercession — The present tense is deliberate: Christ's priestly ministry did not end at the cross. He currently intercedes for his people in the presence of the Father.
once for all — hapax, a single decisive, unrepeatable act. This word will anchor the argument of chapters 9–10.
